summary: Gruber argues that the trend away from skeuomorphic textures in UI design is directly linked to the advent of retina displays. He contends that the heavy textures, shadows, and faux-3D effects of early iOS were essentially compensations for crude, low-resolution screens. With retina displays, these tricks are no longer needed and actually look worse, much like screen-optimized fonts look cheap in print. He points to apps like Letterpress and Twitterrific 5 as exemplars of the new 'flat' design direction, while noting that true flatness isn't the goal — rather, it's honesty and purposefulness in visual design. He draws a parallel to print design history, suggesting that's where software UI is heading.
key_points: Skeuomorphic textures and faux-3D effects were optimizations for low-resolution displays, serving as visual makeup to compensate for crude pixel density
Retina displays eliminate the need for these parlor tricks, enabling honest, print-quality design on screen
The trend isn't truly 'flat' — apps like Letterpress use depth purposefully (e.g., tile shadows when dragging) while rejecting depth as mere decoration
The evolution mirrors typography's progression: from pixel-crafted screen fonts to screen-optimized vector fonts to any font working beautifully on retina
Windows 8's Metro design is forward-thinking in its flatness but suffers from a sterility that prevents it from being endearing
money_quotes: Retina displays are no longer limited in such ways, and need no phony effects to create interfaces that are beautiful.
What Letterpress rejects is not depth, but depth as mere decoration.
The trend away from skeuomorphic special effects in UI design is the beginning of the retina-resolution design era.
They're the aesthetic equivalent of screen-optimized typefaces like Lucida Grande and Verdana. They work on sub-retina displays because sub-retina displays are so crude.
They're an assortment of parlor tricks that fool our eyes into thinking we see something that looks good on a display that is technically incapable of rendering graphic design that truly looks good.
On retina displays, simple, pure, true designs like Letterpress have a bracing effect, like the first sip of an ice-cold beverage on a scorching hot day.
If you want to see the future of software UI design, look to the history of print design.
I think there's a sterility to Metro that prevents it from being endearing. It epitomizes 'flat' design, but I don't think it's great design.
key_insight: Skeuomorphic UI design was a necessary compensation for low-resolution displays, and retina screens are ushering in a print-inspired era where honest, purposeful design replaces visual parlor tricks.
